source.title: Fatal Release of Acetic Acid and Methyl Iodide Mixture at LyondellBasell La Porte Complex

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Acetic Acid and Methyl Iodide Release
actions_taken: Installation of tamper-resistant mechanisms on valve cover fasteners
event.summary: On July 27, 2021, three contract workers employed by Turn2 Specialty Companies were removing an actuator from a pneumatically actuated plug valve in the acetic acid unit at LyondellBasell La Porte Complex. The workers inadvertently removed pressure-retaining nuts while attempting to remove the actuator. When they attempted to remove the coupler using a pry bar, the combination of forces and internal pressure caused the valve cover and plug to eject. Approximately 164,000 pounds of acetic acid mixture at 238°F and 130 psi was released. Two workers were fatally injured from chemical burns and toxic inhalation of acetic acid and methyl iodide vapors. A third Turn2 worker and a LyondellBasell responder were seriously injured. Twenty-nine additional personnel were transported for medical evaluation. Property damage was estimated at $40 million.
recommendations: LyondellBasell: Develop procedures for removing actuating equipment from plug valves, clearly identifying pressure-retaining vs. non-pressure-retaining components; ensure hazardous energy control per 29 CFR 1910.147; conduct risk assessments before authorization; ensure sufficient procedures and safeguards to prevent worker exposure

LyondellBasell: Require competent employees to verify contractor competence, training, and qualifications; ensure contractors are informed of process hazards and provided equipment-specific procedures
Turn2: Develop written, detailed procedures for safely conducting work on process equipment and train employees before work authorization

ASME: Revise Standard B16.34 to require clear marking of pressure-retaining components on existing plug valves and design new plug valves to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components when removing actuators or gearboxes

API: Revise Standard 599 to document multiple incidents, recommend written procedures for actuator/gearbox removal, require clear marking of pressure-retaining components on existing valves, and require new plug valves be designed to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components

VMA Technical Committee: Develop white paper addressing plug valve design with recommendations for written procedures, marking of pressure-retaining components, and design of new plug valves to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components

hazards.name: Pressurized acetic acid and methyl iodide mixture in reactor
Plug valve with pressure-retaining components in pressurized service
Corrosive and toxic chemical mixture (acetic acid and methyl iodide)

threats.name: Inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining bolts from plug valve
Valve cover and plug ejection due to internal pressure
Worker unfamiliarity with valve design and component function
Absence of written procedures for actuator removal
Lack of worker training on actuator removal steps

consequences.name: Catastrophic release of 164,000 pounds of acetic acid mixture
Fatal chemical burns and toxic inhalation injuries to workers
Serious injuries to multiple workers and responders
Exposure of 29 additional personnel to hazardous vapors
Property damage and loss of use estimated at $40 million

controls.name: Plug valve design to prevent pressure-retaining component removal
controls.barrier_role: Prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining bolts during actuator removal
controls.lod_basis: Design feature to eliminate human error in component identification

supporting_text: The design of the plug valve involved in the LyondellBasell incident was of a newer design recommended by the American Petroleum Institute (API) Standard 599 Metal Plug Valves—Flanged, Threaded, and Welding Ends that would allow an actuator to be mounted or removed without affecting the pressure retention of the valve. This newer design, however, did not prevent workers from inadvertently removing pressure retaining components while removing the actuator.
The CSB notes that the design of the plug valves involved in the incidents differed.

controls.name: Clear marking or identification of pressure-retaining components
controls.barrier_role: Enable workers to visually distinguish pressure-retaining bolts from non-pressure-retaining bolts
controls.lod_basis: Visual identification to prevent human error in component selection

supporting_text: One of the workers at the LyondellBasell facility mentioned to the CSB, 'Nothing was marked. None of the bolts were labeled 'remove' [or] 'don't remove.'

The CSB concludes that had the LyondellBasell plug valve pressure-retaining bolts been clearly identified (such as through paint markings or a warning label or sign), the work crew might have removed only the bolts securing the actuator to the plug valve, which would have prevented the incident.

Trevor Kletz states, 'A hardware solution is possible [to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components of valves]. Bolts which can safely be undone when the plant is up to pressure could be painted green; others could be painted red.'

controls.name: Written procedures for actuator removal from plug valves
controls.barrier_role: Provide detailed instructions on which components to remove and which to avoid
controls.lod_basis: Procedural guidance to prevent incorrect component removal

supporting_text: LyondellBasell did not have a procedure detailing how to remove the actuator, and neither LyondellBasell nor Turn2 trained the Turn2 personnel on how to remove the actuator.
LyondellBasell did not provide the work crew with a procedure or instructions on how to remove the actuator from the plug valve.
In hindsight, when asked if they had a written procedure for the task, one Turn2 worker stated, '…not at all. I think that would have been pretty helpful.'

Had LyondellBasell provided a procedure to the Turn2 work crew that detailed the bolts to be removed for the actuator removal work and warned against removing the pressure-retaining bolts, the incident could have been prevented.

controls.name: Worker training on actuator removal procedures
controls.barrier_role: Ensure workers understand correct steps and hazards associated with actuator removal
controls.lod_basis: Knowledge transfer to prevent human error during task execution

supporting_text: Neither LyondellBasell nor Turn2 trained the Turn2 work crew on the steps necessary to remove the actuator.
The OSHA PSM Standard requires that '[t]he contract employer shall assure that each contract employee is trained in the work practices necessary to safely perform his/her job.'
Had the Turn2 work crew been trained on which bolts to remove to safely remove the actuator from the plug valve, the incident might not have occurred.

controls.name: Risk assessment for plug valve actuator removal work
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and determine if depressurization or de-inventorying is required before work
controls.lod_basis: Hazard identification and control measure determination

supporting_text: LyondellBasell personnel did not consider the option of de-inventorying the reactor and connected piping (or otherwise isolating the valve from hazardous energy) before the actuator removal work could be authorized, as the planned actuator removal should not have involved opening the process equipment.

The CSB concludes that in light of the repeated incidents in which workers inadvertently removed pressure-retaining valve components while working to remove actuating equipment, companies should thoroughly assess the risk involved in all plug valve actuator removal work to ensure that sufficient procedures and safeguards are in place to prevent worker exposure to process fluid.

controls.name: Contractor competence verification and oversight
controls.barrier_role: Ensure contractors are qualified and properly supervised during work on process equipment
controls.lod_basis: Management control to verify contractor capability and ensure safe work execution

supporting_text: LyondellBasell did not sufficiently determine that the contractors performing the work were competent, adequately trained, or qualified to perform the actuator removal, and LyondellBasell did not provide sufficient oversight of the actuator removal task.

When contractors are conducting work on or near plant equipment containing hazardous materials, it is critical for the operating company to ensure the contractors are competent and qualified to perform the required work, which could include company competent employees overseeing that the contractor work is conducted in a safe manner.
The CSB did not find documentation indicating the work crew had experience installing, repairing, or removing valves or valve actuators.

At around 6:45 p.m., LyondellBasell issued the work permit for the task, and a LyondellBasell operator told the U.S. Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board (CSB) that he walked with the Turn2 foreman and two Turn2 pipefitters to the acetic acid unit and showed them the actuator they were to remove. The LyondellBasell operator then left the area, and the Turn2 foreman and two Turn2 pipefitters began work to remove the plug valve actuator.

controls.name: Energy isolation and lockout procedures
controls.barrier_role: Control hazardous energy during actuator removal work per OSHA 1910.147
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement for hazardous energy control

supporting_text: LyondellBasell's Energy Isolation Procedure specified that the only pneumatically actuated control valves that were deemed to be approved energy isolation devices were those equipped with manual hand jacks, which could be physically closed and locked.

At the time of the incident, the acetic acid reactor located directly above the plug valve contained approximately 164,000 pounds of a 61.7% acetic acid mixture at a gauge pressure of 130 pounds per square inch (psig) and 238 °F.
Ensure that hazardous energy is controlled when performing these procedures, as required by 29 C.F.R. 1910.147.

controls.name: Detection of incorrect bolt removal during work
controls.barrier_role: Detect when pressure-retaining bolts are being removed before valve failure occurs
controls.lod_basis: Real-time observation and intervention during task execution

supporting_text: The Turn2 employees did not know that removing the nuts shown in Figure 6B was not necessary to remove the actuator; nor did they know that the nuts were pressure-retaining, holding the valve cover in place.

One of the workers explained his reasoning for removing the pressure-retaining nuts shown in Figure 6B. He told the CSB they had to remove the interior fasteners because '…it's all connected. As [the actuator] sits up there, it's all connected to one another.' This worker did not seem to understand that the interior fasteners were pressure-retaining and should not have been removed.

controls.name: Tamper-resistant mechanisms on valve cover fasteners
controls.barrier_role: Prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining fasteners through physical resistance
controls.lod_basis: Design feature to make pressure-retaining component removal difficult

supporting_text: LyondellBasell installed tamper-resistant mechanisms and tags on the valve cover fasteners of actuated plug valves within the unit to help prevent the inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components while the valves are in service.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
